Tool_tds_LearnText: TDS is tax withheld when certain payments are made — salary, FD interest, rent, property, professional fees, and more. Each section has its own rate and threshold. Missing PAN often means a higher rate.
Tool_tds_FormulaNote: Rates and thresholds are illustrative for common sections. Always check the latest Income-tax Act / Finance Act notifications.
Tool_tds_AnnualTaxHint: From your tax calculator / Form 16 estimate. Monthly TDS ≈ annual tax ÷ 12.
Tool_tds_SeniorHint: For 194A, interest threshold is often ₹50,000 instead of ₹40,000.
Tool_tds_AmountHint_192: Used to show net after estimated monthly TDS.
Tool_tds_ThresholdKind_none: Salary uses tax estimate, not a flat %
Tool_tds_Status_Below: Amount is within / at the threshold ({0}). No TDS under this simplified rule.
Tool_tds_ScenarioSubtitle: Same amount, with vs without PAN
Tool_tds_ExampleStep_2: Threshold for non-senior ≈ ₹40,000 — amount is above it.
Tool_tds_ExampleStep_5: Without PAN, rate often jumps to 20%.
Tool_tds_Step_4: Subtract TDS to get net payable / receivable.
Tool_tds_Info_Purpose: Estimate TDS under common Income-tax sections.
Tool_tds_Info_WhyKnow: Wrong section or missing PAN can mean excess withholding and refund delays.
Tool_tds_Info_RealLife: FD maturity, paying rent, buying property, hiring contractors.
Tool_tds_Info_Differentiation: FinYatra groups common TDS sections with PAN / threshold checks in one place.
Tool_atal_pension_LearnText: Atal Pension Yojana (APY) is a government-backed pension for workers in the unorganised sector (and others who enrol). You contribute monthly until 60 based on your entry age and chosen pension (₹1,000–₹5,000). The pension is guaranteed; a spouse pension option applies on death as per scheme rules.
Tool_atal_pension_FormulaNote: Contributions follow the official APY age–pension chart. Eligibility, co-contribution history, and exit rules are set by PFRDA / government notifications.
Tool_atal_pension_InvalidBody: APY contributions in this chart are for entry ages {0}–{1}.
Tool_atal_pension_SummaryBody: Pay about {0}/month for {1} years to receive {2}/month from age 60 (scheme rules apply).
Tool_atal_pension_Step_3: Read monthly contribution from the chart.
Tool_atal_pension_Step_4: Multiply by months until age 60 for total paid.
Tool_atal_pension_Info_Purpose: Find your APY monthly contribution and lifetime contribution total.
Tool_atal_pension_Info_WhyKnow: Starting younger locks a much lower monthly contribution for the same pension.
Tool_atal_pension_Info_RealLife: Gig workers, small business owners, anyone wanting a guaranteed floor pension.
Tool_atal_pension_Info_Alt_Nps_When: Market-linked retirement with higher expected return.
Tool_atal_pension_Info_Alt_Epf_Cons: Not a fixed monthly pension product.
Tool_atal_pension_Info_Alt_Scss_When: Senior citizens needing quarterly interest income.
Tool_atal_pension_Info_Differentiation: FinYatra uses the age–pension contribution chart and shows total paid until 60.
Tool_atal_pension_Info_Motto: A small monthly habit, a guaranteed floor at 60.
Tool_gold_loan_LearnText: A gold loan lets you pledge jewellery for cash without selling it. Lenders typically fund a share of the gold’s market value (LTV). Banks often price lower than NBFCs; tenure is usually short.
Tool_gold_loan_FormulaNote: Max loan = gold value × LTV. Gold value uses 24K-equivalent weight × rate per gram. EMI uses the standard reducing-balance formula. LTV and rates are illustrative — confirm with your lender.
Tool_gold_loan_ValueBody: Estimated gold value ≈ {0} ({1} g pure equivalent). Maximum loan at your LTV ≈ {2}.
Tool_gold_loan_ScenarioSubtitle: Same gold & LTV, bank vs NBFC-style rates
Tool_gold_loan_ExampleStep_1: Weight 50 g at 22K → pure equivalent = 50 × (22/24) ≈ 45.83 g.
Tool_gold_loan_ExampleStep_4: EMI at 10% for 12 months is computed on that loan.
Tool_gold_loan_ExampleStep_5: Compare a slightly lower bank rate vs a higher NBFC rate for EMI impact.
Tool_gold_loan_Step_2: Multiply by the 24K rate to get gold value.
Tool_gold_loan_Step_3: Apply LTV to get the maximum loan.
Tool_gold_loan_Step_4: Compute EMI and total interest for the chosen tenure.
Tool_gold_loan_Info_Purpose: Estimate max loan, EMI, and interest on pledged gold.
Tool_gold_loan_Info_WhyKnow: LTV and rate decide how much cash you get and what it costs — without selling jewellery.
Tool_gold_loan_Info_RealLife: Short-term liquidity, wedding expenses, business working capital, avoiding distress sale of gold.
Tool_gold_loan_Info_Alt_Emi_When: When the loan amount is already fixed.
Tool_gold_loan_Info_Alt_Home_When: Long-term property finance with tax benefits.
Tool_gold_loan_Info_Differentiation: FinYatra links gold valuation, LTV, and bank vs NBFC-style EMI in one tool.
Tool_gold_loan_Info_Motto: Unlock gold value — keep the jewellery.
Tool_capital_gains_tax_Subtitle: LTCG vs STCG by asset type
Tool_capital_gains_tax_LearnText: Capital gains tax depends on the asset and how long you held it. Equity often has a ₹1.25 lakh LTCG exemption; property and gold use longer holding thresholds. Optional Section 54 / 54EC-style exemptions can reduce taxable LTCG.
Tool_capital_gains_tax_FormulaNote: Rates and holding periods are simplified planning figures (illustrative post-Budget rules). Debt MF is treated as always STCG at slab. Confirm with a tax professional for your case.
Tool_capital_gains_tax_ExemptionHint: Optional. Reduces taxable LTCG after any equity ₹1.25L exemption.
Tool_capital_gains_tax_Status_Loss: Sale is at a loss or break-even — no capital gains tax in this simplified view.
Tool_capital_gains_tax_Status_Body: {0}: equity LTCG exemption used ≈ {1}; other exemption ≈ {2}; estimated tax ≈ {3}.
Tool_capital_gains_tax_ScenarioSubtitle: Same prices, shorter vs longer holding
Tool_capital_gains_tax_ExampleTitle: Worked example: equity shares sold after 18 months
Tool_capital_gains_tax_ExampleSubtitle: Buy ₹5 lakh, sell ₹8 lakh, no extra 54 exemption
Tool_capital_gains_tax_ExampleStep_2: Holding 18 months ≥ 12 → treated as LTCG for equity.
Tool_capital_gains_tax_ExampleStep_5: A Section 54/54EC claim would further reduce taxable LTCG.
Tool_capital_gains_tax_Step_2: Classify LTCG vs STCG using asset holding rules.
Tool_capital_gains_tax_Step_3: Apply equity exemption and optional 54/54EC amounts.
Tool_capital_gains_tax_Step_4: Multiply taxable gain by the applicable rate.
Tool_capital_gains_tax_Info_Purpose: Estimate LTCG/STCG tax across common Indian asset types.
Tool_capital_gains_tax_Info_WhyKnow: Holding period and exemptions can change tax by a large margin before you sell.
Tool_capital_gains_tax_Info_RealLife: Selling shares, a flat, gold, or switching debt funds.
Tool_capital_gains_tax_Info_Goal_3: Estimate tax at the right rate
Tool_capital_gains_tax_Info_Alt_Tax_When: Full-year liability including salary and other income.
Tool_capital_gains_tax_Info_Alt_80c_When: Planning deductions under the old regime.
Tool_capital_gains_tax_Info_Differentiat: FinYatra compares asset types, holding paths, and exemption impact in one capital-gains view.
Tool_capital_gains_tax_Info_Motto: Know the tax before you sell.
Tool_section_80c_LearnText: Section 80C lets you claim deductions on eligible investments and expenses up to ₹1.5 lakh under the old tax regime. EPF, PPF, ELSS, life insurance, home-loan principal, NSC, tax-saving FD, and tuition fees commonly count toward the cap.
Tool_section_80c_FormulaNote: Eligible deduction = min(total 80C items, ₹1,50,000). Estimated tax saving ≈ eligible × your marginal slab. New regime generally does not allow 80C — this tool is for old-regime planning.
Tool_section_80c_StatusBody: Total toward 80C ≈ {0}. Eligible deduction ≈ {1}. Estimated tax saving ≈ {2}.
Tool_section_80c_ExampleTitle: Worked example: filling the ₹1.5 lakh cap
Tool_section_80c_ExampleStep_4: Any extra beyond ₹1.5L does not raise the 80C deduction.
Tool_section_80c_ExampleStep_5: Under the new regime, this deduction is generally unavailable.
Tool_section_80c_Step_1: Add all eligible 80C amounts for the year.
Tool_section_80c_Step_3: Multiply eligible amount by your marginal slab.
Tool_section_80c_Step_4: See unused room or amount over the limit.
Tool_section_80c_Info_Purpose: Plan the ₹1.5 lakh old-regime deduction across common instruments.
Tool_section_80c_Info_WhyKnow: Unused limit is lost tax saving; over-investing past ₹1.5L does not raise the deduction.
Tool_section_80c_Info_RealLife: Year-end tax planning, choosing PPF vs ELSS, checking EPF already used.
Tool_section_80c_Info_Goal_3: Estimate tax saving at your slab
Tool_section_80c_Info_Alt_Elss_When: Projecting equity tax-saver returns with lock-in.
Tool_section_80c_Info_Alt_Tax_When: Full old vs new regime comparison.
Tool_section_80c_Info_Differentiation: FinYatra stacks common 80C items against the ₹1.5L cap with a clear tax-saving estimate.
Tool_section_80c_Info_Motto: Fill the cap — don’t overshoot blindly.
Tool_personal_loan_emi_LearnText: Personal loans are unsecured and usually cost more than home or car loans. A shorter tenure cuts total interest but raises EMI. Processing fees add to the true cost even when EMI looks unchanged.
Tool_personal_loan_emi_FormulaNote: EMI uses reducing-balance monthly compounding. Processing fee is an illustrative upfront cost added to effective total — banks may deduct it from disbursement or charge GST separately.
Tool_personal_loan_emi_FeeHint: Optional. Typical range 1–3%. Leave 0 if none.
Tool_personal_loan_emi_FeeBody: Estimated processing fee ≈ {0}. Effective cash cost (EMIs + fee) ≈ {1}.
Tool_personal_loan_emi_ScenarioSubtitle: Same principal and rate, different tenures
Tool_personal_loan_emi_ExampleStep_2: Monthly rate = 14% / 12; EMI uses the standard reducing-balance formula.
Tool_personal_loan_emi_ExampleStep_3: Total payment = EMI × 36; interest = total payment − principal.
Tool_personal_loan_emi_ExampleStep_5: Effective cost ≈ total EMIs + fee — compare offers including fees, not EMI alone.
Tool_personal_loan_emi_Step_1: Enter principal, annual rate, and tenure in years.
Tool_personal_loan_emi_Step_2: Compute monthly EMI on the reducing balance.
Tool_personal_loan_emi_Step_3: Derive total interest and total payment.
Tool_personal_loan_emi_Step_4: Add optional processing fee for effective cost.
Tool_personal_loan_emi_Info_Abbreviation: Personal loan EMI — unsecured instalment loan
Tool_personal_loan_emi_Info_Purpose: Estimate monthly EMI, total interest, and processing-fee cost for a personal loan.
Tool_personal_loan_emi_Info_WhyKnow: Advertised EMI often hides fees and the interest you pay over the full tenure.
Tool_personal_loan_emi_Info_RealLife: Medical bills, wedding expenses, debt consolidation, balance-transfer comparisons.
Tool_personal_loan_emi_Info_Alt_Emi_Exam: Simple EMI on a fixed principal.
Tool_personal_loan_emi_Info_Differentiat: FinYatra shows tenure scenarios and an explicit processing-fee effective-cost note.
Tool_business_loan_emi_LearnText: Business and MSME loans may allow a short moratorium before EMIs start. Interest often accrues in that period and is capitalised into the principal. Plan cash flow for both the holiday and the higher repayment amount.
Tool_business_loan_emi_FormulaNote: Moratorium interest here is simple interest on the original principal for the holiday months, then capitalised. Actual lenders may compound, require interest servicing, or use different Mudra/MSME product rules.
Tool_business_loan_emi_MoratoriumHint: Optional. Simple interest is capitalised before EMI starts. Leave 0 for immediate EMI.
Tool_business_loan_emi_MoratoriumBody: Accrued interest during moratorium ≈ {0}. Repayment principal at EMI start ≈ {1}.
Tool_business_loan_emi_ScenarioSubtitle: Same capitalised principal, different EMI tenures
Tool_business_loan_emi_ExampleStep_4: EMI runs for 5 years on the capitalised amount.
Tool_business_loan_emi_ExampleStep_5: Shorter tenure raises EMI but cuts total interest.
Tool_business_loan_emi_Step_1: Accrue simple interest over moratorium months.
Tool_business_loan_emi_Step_3: Compute EMI for the repayment tenure.
Tool_business_loan_emi_Step_4: Compare shorter vs longer repayment scenarios.
Tool_business_loan_emi_Info_Abbreviation: Business loan EMI — MSME / working-capital instalment
Tool_business_loan_emi_Info_Purpose: Project EMI after an optional moratorium and see total interest.
Tool_business_loan_emi_Info_WhyKnow: A repayment holiday can quietly inflate the amount you repay once EMIs begin.
Tool_business_loan_emi_Info_RealLife: MSME working capital, Mudra-style loans, equipment finance with a short holiday.
Tool_business_loan_emi_Info_Differentiat: FinYatra capitalises moratorium interest clearly before showing EMI and tenure scenarios.
Tool_business_loan_emi_Info_Motto: Plan the holiday and the EMI.
Tool_mutual_fund_returns_LearnText: Mutual fund returns are not guaranteed. This tool projects an assumed annual return for a lumpsum or monthly SIP so you can compare invested amount, estimated value, and gain — then stress-test lower and higher return rates.
Tool_mutual_fund_returns_FormulaNote: Lumpsum uses annual compounding FV. SIP uses the standard future-value-of-annuity formula with monthly rate. Past performance does not guarantee future returns; actual NAV path and costs differ.
Tool_mutual_fund_returns_RateHint: Illustrative assumed CAGR — not a prediction.
Tool_mutual_fund_returns_ExampleStep_1: Choose SIP mode with ₹10,000 per month for 10 years.
Tool_mutual_fund_returns_ExampleStep_3: Estimated value uses the SIP future-value formula at 12% p.a.
Tool_mutual_fund_returns_ExampleStep_5: Compare the same SIP at 10% and 14% to see sensitivity.
Tool_mutual_fund_returns_Step_2: Enter amount, assumed return, and years.
Tool_mutual_fund_returns_Step_3: Compute invested, estimated value, and gain.
Tool_mutual_fund_returns_Step_4: Stress-test lower and higher return rates.
Tool_mutual_fund_returns_Info_Purpose: Estimate corpus and gain for a lumpsum or SIP at an assumed return rate.
Tool_mutual_fund_returns_Info_WhyKnow: A single return assumption can mislead — scenarios show how sensitive your goal is to rate changes.
Tool_mutual_fund_returns_Info_RealLife: Goal planning, SIP vs lumpsum comparison, retirement corpus sketches.
Tool_mutual_fund_returns_Info_Differenti: FinYatra combines lumpsum and SIP in one tool with clear ±2% return scenarios.
Tool_post_office_mis_LearnText: Post Office Monthly Income Scheme (POMIS) pays interest every month on a lump-sum deposit. Interest is not typically compounded into the corpus — you receive it as income, and the principal is returned at maturity (usually 5 years).
Tool_post_office_mis_FormulaNote: Rates are set by the government and change periodically (illustrative default ~7.4%). Deposit limits: ₹9 lakh (single) / ₹15 lakh (joint). Interest is taxable.
Tool_post_office_mis_TenureHint: Standard POMIS tenure is 5 years; principal is returned at maturity.
Tool_post_office_mis_ExampleStep_1: Deposit ₹5,00,000 in Post Office MIS (single account).
Tool_post_office_mis_ExampleStep_4: Over 5 years, total interest paid out ≈ ₹1.85 lakh.
Tool_post_office_mis_ExampleStep_5: At maturity, ₹5 lakh principal is returned.
Tool_post_office_mis_Step_1: Enter deposit within the single/joint limit.
Tool_post_office_mis_Step_4: Principal returns at maturity; interest is taxable.
Tool_post_office_mis_Info_Abbreviation: POMIS — Post Office Monthly Income Scheme
Tool_post_office_mis_Info_Purpose: Estimate monthly MIS income and total interest over the tenure.
Tool_post_office_mis_Info_WhyKnow: POMIS is a popular government income product. Knowing the monthly cash flow and deposit caps helps plan retirement income.
Tool_post_office_mis_Info_RealLife: Retiree monthly income, parking a lump sum safely, comparing with SCSS and senior FDs.
Tool_post_office_mis_Info_Goal_3: Respect single vs joint deposit limits
Tool_post_office_mis_Info_Alt_Scss_When: When you are a senior and prefer quarterly payouts at a higher rate.
Tool_post_office_mis_Info_Alt_Fd_When: When you want bank flexibility or shorter tenures.
Tool_post_office_mis_Info_Alt_Fd_Cons: Rate and safety differ by bank.
Tool_post_office_mis_Info_Alt_Ppf_When: For long-term accumulation, not monthly income.
Tool_post_office_mis_Info_Differentiatio: FinYatra models monthly payout, single/joint caps, and total interest with principal return at maturity.
Tool_post_office_mis_Info_Motto: Steady monthly income from a sovereign scheme.
Tool_flat_vs_reducing_rate_Subtitle: அதே விகித மேற்கோள் — வேறு உண்மைச் செலவு
Tool_flat_vs_reducing_rate_LearnText: Dealers often quote a “flat” interest rate that looks lower than a bank’s reducing-balance rate. Flat interest is charged on the full principal for the entire tenure, so you usually pay more. This tool compares both for the same principal, rate, and tenure.
Tool_flat_vs_reducing_rate_FormulaNote: Flat: total interest = P × rate × years; EMI = (P + total interest) ÷ months. Reducing: standard amortising EMI where interest is charged only on the outstanding balance.
Tool_flat_vs_reducing_rate_RateHint: Enter the quoted annual rate (%) — used for both flat and reducing methods.
Tool_flat_vs_reducing_rate_VerdictNote: At the same quoted {2}, flat interest costs about {0} more over the tenure, and monthly EMI is about {1} higher than reducing balance.
Tool_flat_vs_reducing_rate_RateScenarioS: Extra interest (flat − reducing) at nearby rates
Tool_flat_vs_reducing_rate_ExampleSubtit: Flat vs reducing on the same quote
Tool_flat_vs_reducing_rate_ExampleStep_1: Principal ₹5,00,000; rate 12% p.a.; tenure 3 years (36 months).
Tool_flat_vs_reducing_rate_ExampleStep_4: Reducing EMI uses the standard formula on outstanding balance — lower total interest.
Tool_flat_vs_reducing_rate_ExampleStep_5: The difference in EMI and total interest is the “hidden” cost of a flat quote.
Tool_flat_vs_reducing_rate_Step_1: Enter principal, quoted rate, and tenure.
Tool_flat_vs_reducing_rate_Step_2: Compute flat total interest and flat EMI.
Tool_flat_vs_reducing_rate_Step_3: Compute reducing EMI and total interest.
Tool_flat_vs_reducing_rate_Info_Abbrevia: Flat vs reducing — interest calculation methods
Tool_flat_vs_reducing_rate_Info_Purpose: Show how a flat-rate quote compares with a reducing-balance EMI on the same numbers.
Tool_flat_vs_reducing_rate_Info_WhyKnow: A lower-looking flat rate can cost more than a higher reducing rate. Comparing both protects you from dealer finance traps.
Tool_flat_vs_reducing_rate_Info_RealLife: Car/two-wheeler dealer finance, consumer durables EMI, comparing bank vs dealer offers.
Tool_flat_vs_reducing_rate_Info_Goal_2: See extra interest paid on flat
Tool_flat_vs_reducing_rate_Info_Alt_Emi_: When you already know it is reducing-balance.
Tool_flat_vs_reducing_rate_Info_Alt_Car_: When financing a vehicle with down payment and insurance.
Tool_flat_vs_reducing_rate_Info_Alt_Home: For long-tenure housing loans (almost always reducing).
Tool_flat_vs_reducing_rate_Info_Differen: FinYatra puts flat and reducing side-by-side with clear EMI and interest gaps.
Tool_flat_vs_reducing_rate_Info_Motto: Don’t let a flat quote hide the real cost.
Tool_simple_interest_LearnText: Simple interest grows linearly with time — interest is always calculated on the original principal. It is common in short-term loans, some gold loans, and textbook problems. Compound interest grows faster because interest earns interest.
Tool_simple_interest_FormulaNote: Time can be fractional (e.g. 2.5 years). Amount = principal + simple interest. Compare with annual compounding to see the gap.
Tool_simple_interest_ScenarioSubtitle: Same principal, different time or rate
Tool_simple_interest_ExampleStep_1: Principal P = ₹1,00,000; rate R = 8%; time T = 3 years.
Tool_simple_interest_ExampleStep_4: Interest each year is the same (₹8,000) — no compounding.
Tool_simple_interest_ExampleStep_5: Annual compounding on the same inputs would earn more than ₹24,000.
Tool_simple_interest_Step_1: Enter principal, annual rate %, and time in years.
Tool_simple_interest_Info_Purpose: Calculate simple interest and maturity amount for any principal, rate, and time.
Tool_simple_interest_Info_WhyKnow: Understanding SI vs compound interest helps you read loan offers and short-term deposits correctly.
Tool_simple_interest_Info_RealLife: Gold loans, short-term lending, school/exam problems, comparing with FD compounding.
Tool_simple_interest_Info_Alt_Interest_W: When interest is reinvested or credited to the balance.
Tool_simple_interest_Info_Alt_Fd_When: For bank deposit maturity with compounding frequency.
Tool_simple_interest_Info_Differentiatio: FinYatra keeps the classic SI formula clear and shows a quick compound comparison.
Tool_simple_interest_Info_Motto: Master the simplest interest formula first.
Tool_leave_encashment_LearnText: Leave encashment is payment for unused earned leave. Government employees are generally fully exempt. For others, exemption is limited — this tool uses a practical remaining-lifetime-exemption input (default ₹10 lakh) so you can see how much of the payout may be taxable.
Tool_leave_encashment_FormulaNote: Common payroll formula uses (Basic + DA) ÷ 30 × leave days. Statutory Sec 10(10AA) also caps by 10 months’ salary and earned-leave balance — we simplify to an editable exemption remaining. Confirm with your CA / Form 16.
Tool_leave_encashment_WorkingDaysHint: Most employers use 30. Some use working days in the month.
Tool_leave_encashment_DailyRateHint: If your payslip already shows a daily leave rate, enter it here.
Tool_leave_encashment_ExemptionHint: Lifetime leave-encashment exemption still available. Default ₹10 lakh — lower it if you used exemption earlier.
Tool_leave_encashment_ScenarioSubtitle: Same salary rate, different leave days
Tool_leave_encashment_ExampleStep_4: Exemption remaining ₹10 lakh → entire ₹50,000 is exempt.
Tool_leave_encashment_ExampleStep_5: If amount exceeded remaining exemption, the excess would be taxable.
Tool_leave_encashment_Step_1: Derive daily Basic+DA rate (÷30 or enter daily).
Tool_leave_encashment_Step_3: Apply government full exemption or remaining cap.
Tool_leave_encashment_Info_Purpose: Estimate leave encashment amount and how much may be taxable.
Tool_leave_encashment_Info_WhyKnow: Retirement or resignation leave payouts can create a surprise tax bill if exemption is exhausted.
Tool_leave_encashment_Info_RealLife: Resignation settlement, retirement, or mid-career leave buyout.
Tool_leave_encashment_Info_Alt_Salary_Wh: For full CTC → in-hand, not leave payout.
Tool_leave_encashment_Info_Alt_Gratuity_: For gratuity on exit, not leave cash.
Tool_leave_encashment_Info_Alt_Tax_When: To see how taxable leave adds to annual tax.
Tool_leave_encashment_Info_Differentiati: FinYatra shows amount and a practical exemption-remaining tax split in one place.
Tool_leave_encashment_Info_Motto: Know the payout — and the taxable slice.
Tool_da_LearnText: Dearness Allowance (DA) is a cost-of-living allowance linked to inflation, paid as a percentage of basic pay for many government and PSU employees. Private employers may also use a DA component in salary structure.
Tool_da_FormulaNote: DA% is notified periodically (e.g. central government DA revisions). Enter the % that applies to your pay scale.
Tool_da_Result_TotalFootnote: Often used for HRA / leave / gratuity bases
Tool_da_ExampleStep_5: A 10-point DA hike would add ₹5,000 more DA.
Tool_da_Info_Purpose: Calculate DA amount and Basic+DA from a DA percentage.
Tool_da_Info_WhyKnow: DA revisions change take-home and many salary-linked benefits.
Tool_da_Info_RealLife: Central/state pay revisions, PSU salary, comparing offers with DA.
Tool_da_Info_Differentiation: FinYatra isolates DA% math and side-by-side rate scenarios.
Tool_advance_tax_LearnText: If your estimated annual tax (after TDS) exceeds the threshold, you generally pay advance tax in installments. This tool shows how much should be paid by each due date and whether you have a shortfall versus tax already paid.
Tool_advance_tax_FormulaNote: Schedule shown is the common individual / non-presumptive pattern. Presumptive taxpayers and companies can differ. Interest under 234B/234C is not computed here.
Tool_advance_tax_EstimatedHint: Total income-tax you expect for the year (before considering this schedule).

Tool_loan_against_property_Subtitle: சொத்து மதிப்பு × LTV → அதிகபட்ச கடன் & EMI
Tool_loan_against_property_LearnText: A loan against property (LAP) lets you borrow against residential or commercial property without selling it. Lenders cap the loan at a loan-to-value (LTV) percentage — often around 50–60%. Lower LTV means a smaller loan but may improve approval odds.
Tool_loan_against_property_FormulaNote: Max loan = property value × LTV%. EMI uses standard reducing-balance monthly compounding. Actual LTV, rate, and tenure depend on property type, income, and lender policy.
Tool_loan_against_property_LtvHint: Typical LAP LTV is about 50–60%. Banks may offer less on commercial property.
Tool_loan_against_property_ScenarioSubti: Same property and rate, different LTV
Tool_loan_against_property_ExampleStep_3: EMI on ₹27.5L at 10.5% for 10 years (120 months).

Tool_home_loan_eligibility_LearnText: Banks estimate how much home loan you can service using FOIR (fixed obligation to income ratio). Eligible EMI is a share of monthly income minus existing EMIs. That EMI is then reversed into a maximum principal at your rate and tenure.
Tool_home_loan_eligibility_FormulaNote: Eligible EMI = monthly income × FOIR% − existing EMIs. Max loan uses the reverse EMI formula. Lenders also check credit score, property, and co-applicant income — this is an illustrative FOIR-based estimate.
Tool_home_loan_eligibility_ExistingHint: Include all ongoing loan EMIs. Leave 0 if none.
Tool_home_loan_eligibility_FoirBody: Income {0} × {1} FOIR − existing EMIs {2} → eligible EMI ≈ {3}.
Tool_home_loan_eligibility_ExampleStep_3: Reverse EMI at 8.5% for 20 years to get max principal.
Tool_home_loan_eligibility_ExampleStep_4: Sample EMI on that principal should match eligible EMI closely.
Tool_home_loan_eligibility_ExampleStep_5: Longer tenure raises max loan; stricter FOIR lowers it.

Tool_section_80d_LearnText: Section 80D lets you claim a deduction on health insurance premiums for yourself, your family, and your parents under the old tax regime. Limits rise when you or your parents are senior citizens. Preventive health check-ups (up to ₹5,000) count inside the self/family limit — not on top of it.
Tool_section_80d_FormulaNote: Illustrative old-regime limits: self/family ₹25,000 (₹50,000 if senior); parents ₹25,000 (₹50,000 if senior parents). Preventive checkup ≤ ₹5,000 within the self limit. Estimated tax saving ≈ eligible × your marginal slab. New regime generally does not allow 80D — confirm current rules.
Tool_section_80d_SelfHint: Premium for self, spouse, and dependent children.
Tool_section_80d_LabelSelfSenior: Self / family includes a senior citizen (60+)
Tool_section_80d_PreventiveHint: Capped at ₹5,000 and counted inside the self/family limit.
Tool_section_80d_ExampleStep_1: Self/family limit = ₹25,000 (not senior). Premium ₹20,000 + preventive ₹5,000 = ₹25,000 → eligible ₹25,000.
Tool_section_80d_ExampleStep_2: Parents are senior → limit ₹50,000. Premium ₹30,000 → eligible ₹30,000.
Tool_section_80d_ExampleStep_5: If self were also senior, the self limit would rise to ₹50,000.
Tool_section_80d_Step_1: Apply the self/family limit (₹25k or ₹50k if senior).
Tool_section_80d_Step_2: Add preventive checkup up to ₹5k inside that self limit.
Tool_section_80d_Step_3: Apply the separate parents limit (₹25k or ₹50k if senior).
Tool_section_80d_Step_4: Estimate tax saving as eligible × your slab.
Tool_section_80d_Info_Purpose: Estimate eligible health-insurance deduction and tax saving under Section 80D.
Tool_section_80d_Info_WhyKnow: Self and parents have separate caps; senior status and preventive checkups change how much you can claim.
Tool_section_80d_Info_RealLife: Buying family floater cover, covering parents’ premiums, claiming annual health check-ups.
Tool_section_80d_Info_Goal_3: Estimate tax saving at your slab
Tool_section_80d_Info_Alt_80c_When: When planning PPF, ELSS, NSC, and other ₹1.5L investments.
Tool_section_80d_Info_Alt_Hra_When: When you pay rent and receive HRA.
Tool_section_80d_Info_Alt_Tax_When: Full-year liability including salary and other income.
Tool_section_80d_Info_Differentiation: FinYatra separates self and parents buckets, senior limits, and preventive checkup inside the self cap.
Tool_section_80d_Info_Motto: Insure the family — claim what the law allows.
Tool_nsc_LearnText: National Savings Certificate (NSC) is a government savings product with a fixed 5-year tenure and annual compounding. The purchase amount qualifies under Section 80C (within the overall ₹1.5 lakh cap). Interest is reinvested; in a simplified view, accrued interest in intermediate years may also qualify for 80C.
Tool_nsc_FormulaNote: Maturity = P × (1 + r)^5 with annual compounding. Default rate ~7.7% is illustrative and changes with government notifications. Interest is taxable; 80C treatment of reinvested interest is simplified here — confirm with a tax professional.
Tool_nsc_DepositHint: Purchase amount; counts toward Section 80C (overall ₹1.5L cap).
Tool_nsc_TenureHint: NSC VIII Issue tenure is 5 years.
Tool_nsc_ExampleStep_3: Total interest ≈ ₹44,900, credited and reinvested each year.
Tool_nsc_ExampleStep_5: Interest in years 1–4 is typically treated as reinvested and may qualify for 80C in those years (simplified).
Tool_nsc_Step_2: Apply annual compounding for 5 years: P × (1+r)^5.
Tool_nsc_Step_3: List year-wise interest on the growing balance.
Tool_nsc_Step_4: Note 80C on deposit and simplified intermediate interest.
Tool_nsc_Info_Purpose: Project 5-year NSC maturity and year-wise reinvested interest.
Tool_nsc_Info_WhyKnow: NSC is a popular 80C product with a fixed lock-in; knowing maturity and interest helps compare with PPF and tax-saving FDs.
Tool_nsc_Info_RealLife: Parking a lump sum for 5 years, filling remaining 80C room, gifting NSC to family.
Tool_nsc_Info_Alt_Ppf_Pros: EEE tax treatment; flexible yearly deposits.
Tool_nsc_Info_Alt_Fd_When: When you prefer a bank 5-year lock-in FD.
Tool_nsc_Info_Alt_Fd_Pros: Easy to open at your bank.
Tool_nsc_Info_Alt_Fd_Cons: Interest taxable; rate varies by bank.
Tool_nsc_Info_Alt_80c_When: When combining NSC with PPF, ELSS, and other 80C items.
Tool_nsc_Info_Differentiation: FinYatra shows NSC maturity, year-wise reinvested interest, and a simplified 80C note in one view.
Tool_nsc_Info_Motto: Sovereign savings with a clear 5-year path.
Tool_tax_saving_fd_LearnText: A tax-saving fixed deposit has a mandatory 5-year lock-in and qualifies for Section 80C up to ₹1.5 lakh (old regime, within the overall 80C cap). Interest is taxable. Choose quarterly or annual compounding to see maturity and an estimated tax saving at your slab.
Tool_tax_saving_fd_FormulaNote: Maturity uses A = P × (1 + r/n)^(n×5). 80C eligible = min(deposit, ₹1,50,000). Tax saving ≈ eligible × slab. Interest remains taxable; new regime generally does not allow 80C.
Tool_tax_saving_fd_DepositHint: 80C benefit capped at {0} (overall 80C limit shared with other instruments).
Tool_tax_saving_fd_LockinHint: Tax-saver FDs have a mandatory 5-year lock-in.
Tool_tax_saving_fd_ExampleStep_1: Deposit ₹1,50,000 in a 5-year tax-saver FD at 7%.
Tool_tax_saving_fd_ExampleStep_3: Full ₹1.5 lakh counts under 80C (if room remains under the overall cap).
Tool_tax_saving_fd_ExampleStep_5: Interest earned over 5 years is taxable as per your slab.
Tool_tax_saving_fd_Step_2: Choose annual or quarterly compounding for 5 years.
Tool_tax_saving_fd_Step_4: Estimate 80C tax saving as eligible × slab.
Tool_tax_saving_fd_Info_Purpose: Estimate maturity and 80C tax saving on a 5-year tax-saver FD.
Tool_tax_saving_fd_Info_WhyKnow: Lock-in is fixed at 5 years; compounding and slab decide returns and tax benefit.
Tool_tax_saving_fd_Info_RealLife: Using leftover 80C room at your bank, comparing with ELSS and PPF.
Tool_tax_saving_fd_Info_Goal_3: Estimate tax saving at your slab
Tool_tax_saving_fd_Info_Alt_Elss_When: When you accept market risk for potentially higher returns.
Tool_tax_saving_fd_Info_Alt_Fd_When: When you need flexible tenure without 80C.
Tool_tax_saving_fd_Info_Differentiation: FinYatra combines 5-year lock-in maturity, compounding choice, and 80C tax-saving estimate in one tool.
Tool_tax_saving_fd_Info_Motto: Lock in for five years — claim 80C with clarity.
Tool_retirement_Subtitle: பணவீக்க சரிசெய்த கார்பஸ் மற்றும் SIP திட்டம்
Tool_retirement_LearnText: Your expenses today will cost more by retirement. This tool inflates monthly spend, multiplies by a corpus rule (often 25× annual expenses ≈ 4% withdrawal), subtracts what your existing savings may grow to, and shows the monthly SIP needed to close the gap.
Tool_retirement_FormulaNote: Default multiplier 25 is the classic 4% safe-withdrawal rule. Raise it for a more conservative plan, or lower it if you expect higher post-retirement returns.
Tool_retirement_Result_SipFootnote: At {0} return on the corpus gap
Tool_retirement_ExampleTitle: Worked example: retire at 60 from age 30
Tool_retirement_ExampleStep_4: Grow existing savings at your return rate and subtract from the target.
Tool_retirement_Step_1: Inflate today’s monthly expense to retirement age.
Tool_retirement_Step_3: Grow existing savings to retirement; compute the gap.
Tool_retirement_Info_Purpose: Estimate how large a retirement corpus you need and the SIP to get there.
Tool_retirement_Info_WhyKnow: Under-saving for retirement is hard to fix late; inflation makes today’s expenses look deceptively small.
Tool_retirement_Info_RealLife: Planning EPF/NPS/PPF plus equity SIPs toward a retirement date.
Tool_retirement_Info_Goal_1: See inflation-adjusted monthly spend at retirement
Tool_retirement_Info_Goal_3: Know the SIP needed after existing savings
Tool_retirement_Info_Alt_Fire_When: When you want early financial independence, not only traditional retirement age.
Tool_retirement_Info_Alt_Nps_When: To project National Pension System corpus and annuity.
Tool_retirement_Info_Alt_Nps_Cons: Not a full lifestyle corpus plan.

Tool_retirement_Info_Alt_Epf_Cons: Only one bucket of retirement savings.
Tool_retirement_Info_Differentiation: FinYatra combines inflation, corpus multiplier, existing savings growth, and SIP gap in one retirement view.
Tool_retirement_Info_Motto: Know the corpus — then fund the gap.
Tool_emergency_fund_Subtitle: கவர் மாதங்கள் மற்றும் இலக்கை அடையும் நேரம்
Tool_emergency_fund_LearnText: An emergency fund covers essential expenses if income stops or a large unexpected bill hits. Pick 3–12 months of expenses as your target, subtract what you already have, and see how many months of saving it takes to close the gap.
Tool_emergency_fund_FormulaNote: Park the fund in liquid, low-risk options (savings, liquid funds, short FDs). This tool sizes the target — not investment returns.
Tool_emergency_fund_MonthsHint: 3 months is a start; 6 is common; 12 suits irregular income.
Tool_emergency_fund_SavingsHint: Optional — used only to estimate months to reach the target.
Tool_emergency_fund_Result_MonthsHint: Enter monthly savings to estimate time
Tool_emergency_fund_ExampleStep_5: Compare 3- and 12-month targets to choose a buffer that fits your job risk.

Tool_emergency_fund_Step_4: If saving monthly, months = ceil(gap ÷ savings).
Tool_emergency_fund_Info_Purpose: Size a liquid emergency fund and estimate how long it takes to build.
Tool_emergency_fund_Info_WhyKnow: Without a buffer, a job loss or medical bill can force high-interest debt.
Tool_emergency_fund_Info_RealLife: Salaried households, freelancers, and dual-income families setting a cash reserve.
Tool_emergency_fund_Info_Goal_3: Estimate months to fully fund it
Tool_emergency_fund_Info_Alt_Goal_When: For long-term goals with inflation and SIP rates.

Tool_emergency_fund_Info_Differentiation: FinYatra sizes 3/6/12-month targets and time-to-fund in one simple view.

Tool_crypto_tax_Subtitle: India VDA tax at 30% + optional 1% TDS
Tool_crypto_tax_LearnText: Virtual Digital Assets (crypto) are taxed under a special regime: gains are taxed at a flat 30% with no loss set-off against other income in this simplified view. Transfers may also attract 1% TDS above a threshold.
Tool_crypto_tax_FormulaNote: Illustrative planning figures for Section 115BBH / 194S-style rules. TDS is usually a credit against final tax — confirm thresholds and your exchange’s withholding with a tax professional.
Tool_crypto_tax_LabelTds: Show 1% TDS estimate on sell value
Tool_crypto_tax_TdsHint: Applied when sell value is above {0} (illustrative). Rate {1}.
Tool_crypto_tax_Result_LossNote: Loss — no VDA tax; no set-off in this view
Tool_crypto_tax_Status_Loss: Sale is at a loss or break-even — no 30% VDA tax. Crypto losses generally cannot be set off against other income under this simplified view.
Tool_crypto_tax_Status_Body: Taxable gain ≈ {0} at {1} → tax ≈ {2}. TDS estimate ≈ {3}. Losses cannot be set off against other income here.

Tool_brokerage_Subtitle: Equity delivery vs intraday charge stack
Tool_brokerage_LearnText: Every trade has more than brokerage: STT, exchange fees, and GST on brokerage + exchange. Delivery and intraday use different simplified fee stacks — see total charges and net proceeds.
Tool_brokerage_FormulaNote: Illustrative India equity stacks (simplified). Stamp duty, SEBI charges, and DP fees are omitted. Confirm live rates with your broker and exchange.
Tool_brokerage_TypeHint: Switches default STT and exchange % stacks.

Tool_tcs_Subtitle: Section-wise TCS on remittance, goods & scrap
Tool_tcs_LearnText: TCS is collected by the seller or authorised dealer and remitted to the government. Common cases include foreign remittance under LRS (5% or 20% by amount), sale of goods above ₹50 lakh at 0.1%, and scrap at 1%.
Tool_tcs_FormulaNote: Illustrative planning figures. LRS education/medical exceptions, PAN rules, and FY rate changes are simplified — confirm with your bank or tax professional.
Tool_tcs_NatureHint_lrs_foreign: Simplified: 5% if remittance ≤ ₹7 lakh; 20% if above.
Tool_tcs_AmountHint: Remittance, sale consideration, or scrap sale value.
Tool_tcs_Result_ThresholdFootnote: Where TCS / higher rate kicks in
Tool_tcs_Status_Below: Amount is at or below the threshold of {0} — no TCS in this simplified view.
Tool_tcs_ExampleStep_2: ₹10 lakh is above the ₹7 lakh LRS threshold in this model.
Tool_tcs_ExampleStep_4: If amount were ₹5 lakh (≤ ₹7L), TCS would be 5% = ₹25,000.
Tool_tcs_ExampleStep_5: Sale of goods uses 0.1% only above ₹50 lakh; scrap uses 1% with no threshold here.
Tool_tcs_Step_1: Choose nature: LRS, sale of goods, or scrap.
Tool_tcs_Step_2: Enter the remittance or sale amount.
Tool_tcs_Step_3: Apply the nature’s rate and threshold (LRS uses 5%/20%).
Tool_tcs_Step_4: TCS = amount × rate when applicable; net = amount − TCS.
Tool_tcs_Info_Purpose: Estimate TCS on common India transactions by nature and amount.
Tool_tcs_Info_WhyKnow: Banks and sellers collect TCS upfront — knowing the outflow helps cash planning.
Tool_tcs_Info_RealLife: Sending money abroad under LRS, large B2B goods sales, or scrap dealer invoices.
Tool_tcs_Info_Goal_2: See TCS and net after collection
Tool_tcs_Info_Goal_3: Compare lower vs higher amount scenarios
Tool_tcs_Info_Alt_Tds_When: Tax deducted by payer (salary, interest, rent).
Tool_tcs_Info_Differentiation: FinYatra shows LRS 5%/20%, goods 0.1%, and scrap 1% in one TCS view.
Tool_tcs_Info_Motto: Know what gets collected before you remit or sell.
Tool_tds_on_property_LearnText: When you buy immovable property (other than agricultural land) for more than ₹50 lakh, Section 194IA requires the buyer to deduct 1% TDS on the consideration and deposit it. Joint buyers still deduct on the full amount — this tool shows an equal-share note for planning.
Tool_tds_on_property_FormulaNote: Illustrative 194IA view. Stamp duty value vs agreement value, PAN of seller, and Form 26QB timelines are simplified — confirm with your CA or the Income Tax portal.
Tool_tds_on_property_ConsiderationHint: TDS applies when this exceeds {0} (₹50 lakh).
Tool_tds_on_property_RateHint: Default 1% with PAN; higher if PAN missing (override here).
Tool_tds_on_property_BuyersHint: Joint buyers: TDS is still on full consideration; share is illustrative only.
Tool_tds_on_property_Result_ShareFootnot: Equal split of consideration across {0} buyer(s)
Tool_tds_on_property_Status_Below: Consideration is at or below {0} — no 194IA TDS in this view.
Tool_tds_on_property_Status_Applied: 194IA applies at {0} → TDS ≈ {1}. Deposit via Form 26QB.
Tool_tds_on_property_JointNote: Joint buyers: each may deposit their share of TDS, but the rate applies to the full consideration — not to each share separately for the threshold test.
Tool_tds_on_property_ScenarioSubtitle: At threshold vs your inputs vs joint buyers
Tool_tds_on_property_ExampleStep_4: With 2 joint buyers, TDS is still ₹60,000 on the full amount; equal share of consideration ≈ ₹30 lakh each.
Tool_tds_on_property_ExampleStep_5: Deposit TDS and file Form 26QB within the prescribed timeline.
Tool_tds_on_property_Step_1: Enter consideration (and optional rate / buyer count).
Tool_tds_on_property_Step_4: Joint buyers: note equal share; TDS still on full value.
Tool_tds_on_property_Info_Purpose: Estimate 1% TDS when buying property above ₹50 lakh.
Tool_tds_on_property_Info_WhyKnow: Buyers must deduct and deposit TDS — missing it delays registration and attracts interest.
Tool_tds_on_property_Info_RealLife: Buying a flat or plot above ₹50 lakh, including joint purchases with family.
Tool_tds_on_property_Info_Goal_2: Compute TDS and net to seller

Tool_tds_on_property_Info_Differentiatio: FinYatra focuses on 194IA with threshold, rate override, and joint-buyer share.
Tool_tds_on_property_Info_Motto: Deduct 1% before you register the sale.
Tool_tds_on_rent_LearnText: Individuals and HUFs (not subject to tax audit) paying rent above ₹50,000 a month must deduct 2% TDS under Section 194IB and deposit it using Form 26QC. This tool annualises monthly TDS for cash planning.
Tool_tds_on_rent_FormulaNote: Illustrative 194IB view. Company/firm tenants may use 194I instead. Confirm due dates and Form 26QC on the Income Tax portal.
Tool_tds_on_rent_RentHint: TDS applies when monthly rent exceeds {0}.
Tool_tds_on_rent_RateHint: Default 2% (194IB). Override if your case differs.
Tool_tds_on_rent_Status_Below: Monthly rent is at or below {0} — no 194IB TDS in this view.
Tool_tds_on_rent_Form26qcNote: Deposit TDS and file Form 26QC (challan-cum-statement) for each month as required.
Tool_tds_on_rent_ScenarioSubtitle: At threshold vs your rent vs +25%
Tool_tds_on_rent_ExampleStep_5: File Form 26QC and pay TDS for each applicable month.
Tool_tds_on_rent_Step_4: Remember Form 26QC for deposit and reporting.
Tool_tds_on_rent_Info_Purpose: Estimate 2% TDS when monthly rent exceeds ₹50,000.
Tool_tds_on_rent_Info_WhyKnow: Tenants must deduct TDS monthly — missing Form 26QC attracts interest and fees.
Tool_tds_on_rent_Info_RealLife: Paying high house or office rent as an individual or HUF tenant.
Tool_tds_on_rent_Info_Differentiation: FinYatra annualises 194IB TDS and flags Form 26QC in one rent view.
Tool_tds_on_rent_Info_Motto: If rent crosses ₹50k, plan the 2% TDS.
Tool_goal_sip_LearnText: Enter your goal amount, time horizon, and expected return. The calculator works backwards to the monthly SIP you need using the standard annuity formula.
Tool_goal_sip_FormulaNote: Uses sipRequired from compound interest math. Higher expected returns lower the required SIP — but returns are not guaranteed.
Tool_goal_sip_RateHint: Equity SIPs often use 10–14% illustrative CAGR; debt lower.
Tool_goal_sip_Result_TargetFootnote: Corpus you want at the end
Tool_goal_sip_ScenarioSubtitle: Same goal — how SIP changes with return
Tool_goal_sip_ExampleStep_2: Assume 12% p.a. expected return → monthly rate r = 1%.
Tool_goal_sip_ExampleStep_4: You get the monthly amount to invest every month.
Tool_goal_sip_ExampleStep_5: Compare lower/higher return scenarios — lower return needs a larger SIP.
Tool_goal_sip_Step_1: Convert annual return to monthly rate r.
Tool_goal_sip_Step_4: Total invested = SIP × n (before market gains).
Tool_goal_sip_Info_Abbreviation: Goal SIP — SIP needed for a target
Tool_goal_sip_Info_Purpose: Find the monthly SIP that can grow to your goal amount.
Tool_goal_sip_Info_WhyKnow: Guessing a SIP amount often undershoots the goal. Working backwards from the target is clearer.
Tool_goal_sip_Info_RealLife: House down payment, wedding corpus, child education fund.
Tool_goal_sip_Info_Goal_2: See total amount you will invest
Tool_goal_sip_Info_Alt_Sip_When: When you know the SIP and want future value.

Tool_goal_sip_Info_Alt_Lumpsum_When: When you invest once instead of monthly.

Tool_goal_sip_Info_Motto: Start from the goal — then size the SIP.
Tool_stp_LearnText: Park a lumpsum in a debt fund, then transfer a fixed amount every month into equity. You stay invested in both while rupee-cost averaging into equity.
Tool_stp_FormulaNote: Each month debt and equity earn their assumed returns, then the STP amount moves from debt to equity (capped by debt balance).
Tool_stp_ScenarioSubtitle: Same STP schedule, different equity returns
Tool_stp_ExampleStep_1: Start with ₹5,00,000 in a debt fund.
Tool_stp_ExampleStep_2: Transfer ₹10,000 every month to equity for 24 months.
Tool_stp_ExampleStep_3: Debt balance earns ~6%; equity instalments grow at ~12%.
Tool_stp_ExampleStep_4: After 24 months, see equity corpus + leftover debt.
Tool_stp_ExampleStep_5: Compare with investing the full lumpsum in equity at once (higher risk).
Tool_stp_Step_1: Grow debt and equity by monthly returns.
Tool_stp_Step_2: Move min(STP, debt balance) into equity.
Tool_stp_Step_4: Report equity value, debt residual, and total.
Tool_stp_Info_Purpose: Project debt→equity monthly transfers and ending balances.
Tool_stp_Info_WhyKnow: STPs reduce timing risk vs investing a lumpsum in equity on day one.
Tool_stp_Info_RealLife: Bonus or FD maturity moved gradually into equity funds.
Tool_stp_Info_Alt_Sip_When: When money comes from salary, not a parked lumpsum.
Tool_stp_Info_Alt_Sip_Pros: No need for a large starting corpus.
Tool_stp_Info_Alt_Swp_When: When you withdraw from a corpus instead of transferring.
Tool_stp_Info_Alt_Lumpsum_When: When you accept full market timing risk.
Tool_stp_Info_Alt_Lumpsum_Example: Invest entire ₹5 lakh in equity on day one.
Tool_stp_Info_Alt_Lumpsum_Pros: Maximum time in market if markets rise.
Tool_stp_Info_Differentiation: FinYatra simulates month-by-month debt and equity balances with a clear residual debt view.
Tool_stp_Info_Motto: Transfer steadily — stay invested in both.
Tool_gold_investment_Subtitle: SIP அல்லது மொத்த தங்கம் — டிஜிட்டல் vs உடல்
Tool_gold_investment_LearnText: Project gold’s future value with SIP or lumpsum at an assumed appreciation rate. Physical jewellery often loses making charges upfront; digital gold / ETFs invest closer to the full amount.
Tool_gold_investment_FormulaNote: Digital uses full SIP/lumpsum FV. Physical reduces the invested amount by making charges %, then compounds at the same gold appreciation.
Tool_gold_investment_AppreciationHint: Long-term gold returns vary; 6–10% is a common planning band.
Tool_gold_investment_MakingHint: Typical jewellery making charges ~3–15% depending on design.
Tool_gold_investment_CompareNote: At {0} making charges, you pay about {1} extra on physical — digital ends ahead by roughly {2} in this projection.
Tool_gold_investment_BreakdownSubtitle: Assuming full amount invested (no making charges)
Tool_gold_investment_ExampleStep_2: Digital gold compounds the full SIP at 8% p.a.
Tool_gold_investment_ExampleStep_3: Physical jewellery: ~8% making means only ~₹4,600 buys gold each month.
Tool_gold_investment_ExampleStep_4: Compare ending digital vs physical values.
Tool_gold_investment_ExampleStep_5: For jewellery, also weigh emotional/use value — not only returns.
Tool_gold_investment_Step_2: Grow digital investment at gold appreciation.
Tool_gold_investment_Step_3: For physical, reduce principal by making % first.
Tool_gold_investment_Step_4: Compare digital vs physical ending values.

Tool_gold_investment_Info_Motto: Gold can hedge — making charges still cost.
Tool_credit_card_emi_Subtitle: குறைவுநிலை EMI மற்றும் பிளாட் வீத ஒப்பீடு
Tool_credit_card_emi_LearnText: Convert a purchase into EMI using the reducing-balance formula. Enter a monthly or annual rate — many cards quote monthly %. Compare with the flat-rate method dealers sometimes use, so you see the real interest cost.
Tool_credit_card_emi_FormulaNote: Reducing EMI uses standard amortisation. Flat interest = P × annual% × years. “No-cost EMI” often hides interest in a higher MRP or processing fee.
Tool_credit_card_emi_VerdictBody: Reducing EMI ≈ {0}/month with about {1} interest. Flat method would charge about {2} interest on the same quote.
Tool_credit_card_emi_ExampleStep_1: Purchase ₹50,000; tenure 12 months; monthly rate 1.5% → 18% p.a.
Tool_credit_card_emi_ExampleStep_2: Reducing EMI uses the standard formula on outstanding balance.
Tool_credit_card_emi_ExampleStep_3: Total payment = EMI × 12; interest = total − principal.
Tool_credit_card_emi_ExampleStep_5: Compare flat vs reducing to spot the higher flat cost.
Tool_credit_card_emi_Step_1: Enter purchase amount and tenure in months.
Tool_credit_card_emi_Step_2: Enter monthly or annual rate (convert monthly × 12).
Tool_credit_card_emi_Step_3: Compute reducing EMI, total interest, and total payment.
Tool_credit_card_emi_Step_4: Compare with flat-rate interest and EMI.
Tool_credit_card_emi_Info_Abbreviation: CC EMI — purchase to equated monthly instalments
Tool_credit_card_emi_Info_Purpose: Estimate EMI, interest, and flat vs reducing cost on a card purchase.
Tool_credit_card_emi_Info_WhyKnow: Card EMI and “no-cost” offers can hide high effective rates. Comparing methods protects you.
Tool_credit_card_emi_Info_RealLife: Converting a phone or appliance purchase to EMI; checking a no-cost EMI quote.
Tool_credit_card_emi_Info_Goal_3: Use monthly or annual rate input
Tool_credit_card_emi_Info_Alt_Flat_When: When you only need a side-by-side flat vs reducing compare.
Tool_credit_card_emi_Info_Alt_Emi_When: For a generic loan EMI without flat compare.
Tool_credit_card_emi_Info_Alt_Personal_W: When financing via a personal loan instead of card EMI.
Tool_credit_card_emi_Info_Differentiatio: FinYatra shows reducing EMI plus a flat comparison and monthly/annual rate toggle for card purchases.
Tool_credit_card_emi_Info_Motto: Know the real EMI cost — not just the monthly number.
Tool_emi_to_rate_LearnText: You know the loan amount, EMI, and tenure — but not the rate. This tool solves the reducing-balance EMI formula for the implied annual interest rate. Useful for “no-cost EMI” and opaque dealer quotes.
Tool_emi_to_rate_FormulaNote: Uses binary search on the standard EMI formula. If EMI × months is less than principal, the schedule is impossible at a non-negative rate.
Tool_emi_to_rate_MonthsHint: Use the EMI tenure from your statement or offer letter.
Tool_emi_to_rate_VerdictBody: Your EMI implies about {0} p.a., with roughly {1} total interest over the tenure.
Tool_emi_to_rate_VerdictImpossible: EMI × months is less than principal — that schedule cannot amortise at a non-negative interest rate. Check the inputs.
Tool_emi_to_rate_ScenarioSubtitle: How a slightly different EMI changes the implied rate
Tool_emi_to_rate_ExampleStep_4: Binary-search annual rate until reducing EMI matches ₹4,800.
Tool_emi_to_rate_ExampleStep_5: The solved rate is the effective interest baked into the offer.
Tool_emi_to_rate_Step_1: Enter principal, monthly EMI, and tenure months.
Tool_emi_to_rate_Step_2: If EMI ≤ principal / months, rate is 0% (or impossible if lower).
Tool_emi_to_rate_Step_3: Binary-search annual rate so computed EMI matches input.
Tool_emi_to_rate_Step_4: Show implied rate, total interest, and total payment.
Tool_emi_to_rate_Info_Abbreviation: EMI to rate — reverse interest calculator

Tool_emi_to_rate_Info_RealLife: No-cost EMI on electronics; dealer finance; checking a loan offer letter.

Tool_emi_to_rate_Info_Motto: If they won’t quote the rate — reverse it from the EMI.
Tool_loan_balance_transfer_Subtitle: தற்போதைய கடன் vs புதிய வீதம் (கட்டணம் உடன்)
Tool_loan_balance_transfer_LearnText: A balance transfer moves your outstanding to a lower-rate lender. You pay a processing fee, then a new EMI. This tool compares remaining interest, net savings after fee, and break-even months until the fee is recovered.
Tool_loan_balance_transfer_FormulaNote: Assumes same outstanding and remaining tenure on both loans. Check foreclosure charges, legal fees, and whether the new lender resets tenure.
Tool_loan_balance_transfer_FeeHint: Typical BT fee is about 0.5–2% of outstanding (illustrative).
Tool_loan_balance_transfer_VerdictYes: Transfer looks worthwhile — about {0} net savings. Fee recovers in roughly {1} months via lower EMI.
Tool_loan_balance_transfer_VerdictNo: Fee of {0} may wipe out interest savings of {1}. Re-check rates, tenure, and other charges.
Tool_loan_balance_transfer_ExampleStep_2: Compute current EMI and remaining interest.
Tool_loan_balance_transfer_ExampleStep_3: New rate 8.2% → new EMI and interest; fee = 1% of outstanding.
Tool_loan_balance_transfer_ExampleStep_5: Break-even months ≈ fee ÷ (current EMI − new EMI).

Tool_loan_balance_transfer_Step_2: Enter new rate and processing fee %.
